attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a painless swelling that has been present at her posterior left shoulder for about ten years and does not bother her unduly. She is otherwise healthy with no significant family or past medical history. On examination, there a regular, well defined mass with a 10cm diameter, that feels soft, fluctuant and even (Image). A slippage sign is elicited. There is no mobility abnormality of her shoulders or her necks. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This swelling is most likely to be a lipoma. Its appearance and the length of the history virtually confirm the diagnosis. Lipomas are slow-growing subcutaneous tumours composed of adipose cells that most commonly occur on the trunk, but can be found at any site on the body. To rule out malignancy, especially in large lipomas, investigations including physical examination, ultra-sound and a skin biopsy should be done. Invasive management options for disturbing simple lipomas include surgical excision, the squeeze technique, or liposuction.

Liposarcomas almost never arises in the skin. They are deep tumours, and which often grow on the thigh, groin or at back of the abdomen.

Lymphoceles are benign unilocular neck cysts that do not fit the location of this presentation.

A winged scapula can be associated with a ruptured trapezius and there will be interruptions to the range of motion of the neck e.g. rotation, extension and lateral bend.

Axillary lymphadenopathy would present less gradually as its causes are commonly infective or malignant.
learningPoints: The slippage sign or slip sign is classical of lipomas where the swelling will slip away from the palpation point.
references: Characteristic imaging findings in lymphoceles of the head and Neck : American Journal of roentgenology : Vol. 197, no. 6 (AJR) (no date) American Journal of Roentgenology. Available at: https://www.ajronline.org/doi/10.2214/AJR.10.6278 (Accessed: December 26, 2022).

Ferrer, R. (1998) Lymphadenopathy: Differential diagnosis and evaluation, American Family Physician. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/1998/1015/p1313.html (Accessed: December 26, 2022).
Lipoma and Liposarcoma. DermNet. (n.d.). Retrieved March 17, 2023, from https://dermnetnz.org/topics/lipoma-and-liposarcoma
Lipoma pathology - statpearls - NCBI bookshelf (no date). Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482343/ (Accessed: December 26, 2022).
